title: Garage door insulation: is it worth it?
description: Garage door insulation costs about £70 DIY but only pays off if you heat the space. Real UK figures for workshops, home gyms and integral garages.
blurb: A single-skin steel door leaks heat five times faster than a wall. When insulating it pays, and when it does not.
lede: A standard British garage door is a sheet of steel about a millimetre thick, hanging loosely in a frame with daylight visible round the edges. As a thermal barrier it is roughly as effective as a biscuit tin lid. If you have turned your garage into a workshop, gym or home office, that one door is probably losing more heat than any wall in your house. Insulation kits cost £50 to £80 and a DIY job with rigid foam boards costs about the same. The question is whether your garage is the kind where the spend ever comes back.
short_answer: Garage door insulation is worth it only if you heat the garage. For a workshop or gym used 15 hours a week, 25mm of foam board on a single-skin steel door saves roughly £23 a year at current electricity prices, paying back a £70 DIY job in about three years. Heat the space more and payback drops below a year. If the garage is detached and unheated, you will save nothing on bills, though the door still benefits from draught-proofing if you store anything sensitive to damp.

sections.h2: Why a steel garage door loses heat so fast
sections.html:
Heat loss through any surface is measured by its U-value: the watts that escape through each square metre for every degree of temperature difference. Lower is better. Some context:

  • A modern insulated loft: around 0.15 W/m²K
  • An insulated cavity wall: around 0.5 W/m²K
  • Single glazing: around 4.8 W/m²K
  • A single-skin steel up-and-over garage door: 5.5 to 6 W/m²K

That makes the typical garage door the single worst thermal element on most British properties, leakier than a Victorian sash window. A standard single door is about 2.1m wide by 2m high, so you have roughly 4.2 square metres of bare metal radiating heat to the street.

Conduction through the panel is only half the story. A garage door is a moving panel sitting in a loose frame, with gaps down both sides, across the top and along the bottom. Cold air pours through those gaps whether or not the panel itself is insulated, which is why any sensible plan tackles both the panel and the perimeter. The same principle applies all over the house, which is why draught-proofing is consistently one of the cheapest wins in energy saving.

Adding 25mm of foil-faced PIR board (the rigid foam used in building work) takes the panel from around 5.5 W/m²K down to roughly 1.3 W/m²K once you allow for the steel bracing bridging through. That is a cut of more than three quarters in conducted heat loss through the door.

sections.h2: The first question: do you heat the garage?
sections.html:
This is the whole decision, so be honest about it. Garages fall into three camps.

Detached and unheated

If nobody ever heats the space, there is no heating energy to save and insulating the door will not cut a penny off your bills. It still moderates temperature swings, which reduces condensation on tools, bikes and stored boxes, and it makes the garage less brutal to work in on a cold Saturday. Those are comfort and preservation benefits, not financial ones. Spend the money where you do heat instead.

Heated workspace

A garage used as a workshop, gym, studio or office, warmed with a fan heater, panel heater or radiator, is where insulation genuinely pays. Almost all garage heating is electric, which at 26.11p per kWh is the most expensive heat in the house, so every kilowatt-hour you stop leaking through the door is worth more than three times the same saving on gas central heating. If you are choosing how to heat the space in the first place, our guide to electric heater types covers which kind suits an occasional-use room.

Integral, attached to the house

An integral garage under a bedroom or beside a hallway acts as a cold buffer zone. The house leaks heat into the garage through the party wall and ceiling, and the garage leaks it onwards through the door. Insulating the door and sealing its gaps raises the garage temperature a few degrees, which slows the loss from the rooms next to it. The effect is real but modest, perhaps £10 to £25 a year. The bigger wins in an integral garage are insulating the ceiling below the room above and the wall shared with the house, for the same reason loft insulation beats almost everything else: you insulate as close to the heated space as you can.

Take a standard 4.2 m² single door, a winter average outdoor temperature of around 7°C, and the heating patterns people actually use. The table compares the heat conducted out through a bare steel door (U-value 5.5) with the same door carrying 25mm of PIR board (effective U-value about 1.3), costed at the current electricity cap rate.

How the garage is usedLost through bare steel doorWith 25mm PIR fittedSaving per year
Workshop or gym, about 15 hours a week at 18°C, October to April114 kWh (£30)27 kWh (£7)£23
Home office or daily gym, about 40 hours a week at 18°C305 kWh (£80)72 kWh (£19)£61
Held at 12°C all winter (paint store, animals, frost protection)648 kWh (£169)153 kWh (£40)£129
Detached and never heated0 kWh0 kWh£0 on bills

At 26.11p per kWh, Ofgem price cap July to September 2026. Door area 4.2 m², standard single up-and-over door; figures are door losses only, rounded.

Against a £50 to £80 outlay, the occasional workshop pays back in around three years, a daily-use room in just over one, and a continuously heated garage within a single winter. These figures cover only the door panel; the same heated garage is also losing heat through an uninsulated roof, walls and floor, so treat the door as the first job rather than the whole job.

Two honest caveats. First, the bare-door figures assume you actually hold the target temperature; in practice many people give up because a 2kW fan heater cannot keep pace with a single-skin garage, and there insulation buys comfort rather than a measurable saving. Second, if your garage heating runs on a cheap overnight tariff, the savings shrink in proportion to your unit rate.

sections.h2: How to insulate a garage door properly
sections.html:
There are three routes, in ascending order of cost.

DIY with PIR board, about £60 to £80

Two sheets of 25mm foil-faced PIR (each 2.4m by 1.2m, roughly £25 a sheet), a serrated knife, foil tape and some high-grab adhesive will cover a single door. Cut panels to fit snugly between the steel bracing ribs, stick them to the inner face, and tape the joints with foil tape so the surface is sealed. The foil facing matters: it reflects radiant heat and stops warm moist air reaching the cold steel behind, where it would otherwise condense.

A purpose-made kit, about £50 to £80

Kits sold for garage doors are usually foil-faced bubble layers or thin expanded polystyrene panels with fixing tape. They are quicker and tidier than cutting PIR and fine for the job, but most are thinner than 25mm board, so expect somewhat less benefit per pound. Compare the quoted thickness and material before assuming a kit beats DIY.

A replacement insulated door, £1,000 to £2,500 fitted

Modern insulated sectional and roller doors have foam-filled panels with U-values of 1.0 to 1.5 and proper perimeter seals built in. The performance is excellent, but as an energy measure alone it can never repay four figures. Buy one when the existing door is worn out anyway, and treat the insulation as a free upgrade that comes with it.

Three practical warnings

  • Weight. Up-and-over doors are balanced by springs set for the original panel weight. 25mm PIR over a single door adds only 3 to 4kg, which is fine, but heavier materials can make the door drop like a guillotine or refuse to stay open. If the door feels heavy afterwards, the springs need adjusting by someone who knows how, because they store a lot of energy.
  • Movement. A one-piece up-and-over panel twists slightly as it swings. Fit insulation as separate boards between the bracing ribs rather than one rigid slab, and keep clear of the lock, cables and lifting arms.
  • Ventilation. Do not seal the garage airtight. Cars, paint, mowers and damp bikes all release moisture and fumes, and a sealed cold box grows mould. Keep any existing vents clear, and if damp appears on the walls afterwards, add ventilation rather than removing the insulation.

sections.h2: The gaps round the edge matter as much as the panel
sections.html:
In a typical garage, the air leaking round the door can carry away as much heat as the steel conducts through it. The fixes are cheap and work whether or not you insulate the panel:

  • Bottom seal: a rubber strip screwed or stuck to the floor or the door's bottom edge closes the biggest gap, the one you can usually see daylight through. £10 to £20.
  • Side and top brush strips: screw-on brush or rubber-blade seals follow the door's movement without jamming it. £15 to £30 for a set.
  • The pedestrian door and window: if the garage has a side door or window, standard draught strips finish the job for a few pounds.

For a heated garage, do the seals first. They cost less than the panel insulation, take half an hour, and also stop wind-driven rain and leaves blowing in. They are the same products and techniques as the rest of the house, covered in our draught-proofing guide.

One scenario deserves a special mention: garages with water pipes in them, often feeding an outside tap or a utility sink. A sealed and insulated door significantly cuts the risk of a freeze, but the pipes themselves should be lagged regardless; see pipe lagging and frozen pipes for the ten-minute version of that job.

Garage door insulation is one of those measures where the answer is binary rather than vague. Heat the garage, even occasionally? Then yes: £70 of foam board and £25 of seals will pay back in one to three years, the room will reach temperature faster, hold it with a smaller heater, and be far more usable in January. Never heat it? Then no: insulate nothing, but consider £20 of draught seals anyway if damp tools or stored furniture bother you.

Order of work for a heated garage: seal the door perimeter, insulate the door panel, then look at the roof and walls if you use the space heavily. For an integral garage, insulate the surfaces shared with the house before touching the door. And keep the door in perspective: it is the worst single element in the building, but the cheapest heat is still the heat you do not need, so set the garage thermostat low and treat the space as a cool room rather than a warm one. For everything upstream of the garage, our heating guides cover where the rest of the bill goes.

faq.q: Will insulation make my garage door too heavy?
faq.a: Not if you use lightweight boards. 25mm PIR across a single door adds only 3 to 4kg, which the balance springs absorb without trouble. Avoid heavy materials such as plasterboard or thick timber, and if the door feels noticeably heavier or will not stay open afterwards, have the spring tension adjusted professionally rather than leaving it straining.

faq.q: What thickness of insulation should I use on a garage door?
faq.a: 25mm foil-faced PIR board is the sweet spot for most doors. It cuts conducted heat loss by more than three quarters, weighs little, and on most up-and-over and sectional doors it sits between the bracing ribs without fouling the mechanism. Going thicker gives diminishing returns and risks catching on the frame or lifting gear as the door moves.

faq.q: Is it worth insulating the door of an unheated detached garage?
faq.a: Not for your energy bills, because there is no heating energy to save. It does reduce temperature swings and condensation, which helps if you store tools, bikes or anything that rusts. For an unheated garage, £20 of draught seals to keep wind and rain out is usually the better buy than panel insulation.

faq.q: Should I just buy a new insulated garage door instead?
faq.a: Only when the old door needs replacing anyway. A fitted insulated sectional door costs £1,000 to £2,500 and performs very well, but the energy saved can never repay that on its own when £70 of DIY boards and seals captures most of the same benefit. Treat built-in insulation as a free bonus at natural replacement time.

faq.q: Does insulating the garage door help a cold bedroom above the garage?
faq.a: A little, because a warmer garage drains less heat from the room above it. But the bigger fix is insulating the garage ceiling directly under that bedroom floor, which acts on the problem at source. Do the ceiling first, then the door and its draught seals as the finishing step.
